Hello dear friend seeking help with date analysis tasks using python code today. We shall build a function that filters historical records based on century specifications. The core logic involves calculating the start and end years for any given century number. A century begins one year after a multiple of one hundred and ends at the next multiple. For instance, the nineteenth century spans from one thousand eight hundred one through one thousand nine hundred. This rule applies universally to all centuries in the gregorian calendar system we use. Our solution must parse the year component from each date string provided in the input list. The input format is strictly defined as year month day separated by hyphens. We will extract the first four characters to get the year value for comparison. Once we have the year, we check if it falls between the calculated start and end values. If the year matches the range, we keep the original date string in our result list. This approach ensures we preserve the exact formatting of the input data without modification. Efficiency is crucial when dealing with massive datasets containing thousands or millions of entries. We avoid unnecessary string conversions or complex date object creation to maintain speed. Simple integer arithmetic and string slicing operations are very fast in modern python environments. The function will iterate through the list once, performing constant time checks for each item. This results in linear time complexity which is optimal for this type of filtering task. Let us define the start year as the century number minus one multiplied by one hundred plus one. The end year is simply the century number multiplied by one hundred. These formulas give us the precise boundaries for inclusion in our final output. We must handle edge cases where the input list might be empty or contain invalid date formats. Although the prompt assumes valid input, robust code often includes basic error handling for safety. However, to keep the solution efficient and focused, we assume all inputs follow the specified pattern. The function returns a new list containing only the matching dates in their original order. This preserves the chronological sequence if the input was already sorted by time. Researchers can then use this filtered list for further statistical analysis or visualization purposes. The code is designed to be easily integrated into larger data processing pipelines or research scripts. You can call this function with any list of dates and any century integer you require. Just remember that the century parameter refers to the ordinal number like nineteen for the one eight hundreds. It does not refer to the year range starting with one nine zero zero.
